coredata.dc:title: Sonocatalytic degradation of methylene blue by a novel graphene quantum dots anchored CdSe nanocatalyst
coredata.dc:creator: Sajjadi, Saeed
Khataee, Alireza
Kamali, Mehdi
coredata.dc:description: Cadmium selenide/graphene quantum dots (CdSe/GQDs) nanocatalyst with small band gap energy and a large specific surface area was produced via a facile three-step sonochemical-hydrothermal process. The features of the as-prepared CdSe, GQDs and CdSe/GQDs samples were characterized by photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), energy dispersive X-ray (EDX), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transformed infrared (FT-IR), diffuse-reflectance spectrophotometer (DRS), and Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) analysis. The sonocatalytic activity of the synthesized CdSe/GQDs was effectively accelerated compared with that of pure CdSe nanoparticles in degradation of methylene blue (MB). The influence of the CdSe/GQDs dosage (0.25–1.25g/L), initial MB concentration (20–30mg/L), initial solution pH (3–12), and ultrasonic output power (200–600W/L) were examined on the sonocatalytic treatment of MB aqueous solutions. The degradation efficiency (DE%) of 99% attained at 1g/L of CdSe/GQDs, 20mg/L of MB, pH of 9, and an output power of 200W/L at 90min of ultrasonic irradiation. Furthermore, DE% increased with addition of K2S2O8 and H2O2 as the enhancers via producing more free radicals. However, addition of sulfate, carbonate, and chloride as radical sweeper decreased DE%. Furthermore, well-reusability of the CdSe/GQDs sonocatalyst was demonstrated for 5 successive runs and some of the sonocatalytic generated intermediates were indicated by GC–MS analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cadmium selenide
Graphene quantum dots
Methylene blue
Nanocatalyst
Sonocatalytic degradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Impact of the main operational parameters on the sonocatalysis processes
Impact of the content of GQDs
Impact of the presence of radical scavengers
Impact of the presence of various enhancers

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The optical images of (a) the concentrated GQDs solution and (b) the attenuated GQDs solution excited by 360nm.
SEM images of (a) unmodified CdSe nanoparticles and (b) 2.5wt% CdSe/GQDs nanoparticles. EDX spectra of (c) unmodified CdSe and (d) 2.5wt% CdSe/GQDs nanoparticles.
(a) XRD patterns, (b) FT-IR spectra, and (c) BET isotherms of unmodified CdSe and 2.5wt% CdSe/GQDs nanoparticles.
Comparison of sonolysis alone with sonocatalysis processes, (pH = 9, [MB]0 =20mg/L, [Catalyst]=1g/L and power=200W/L).
(Ahυ)2–hυ curves of (a) the unmodified CdSe and (b) 2.5wt% CdSe/GQDs nanoparticles; and (c) Impact of GQDs content on the degradation of MB; (pH = 9, [MB]0 =20mg/L, [Catalyst]=1g/L and power=200W/L).

Impacts of (a) sonocatalyst dosage (pH = 9, [MB]0 =20mg/L, and power=200W/L); (b) initial MB concentration (pH = 9, [Catalyst]=1g/L and power=200W/L); (c) pH ([MB]0 =20mg/L, [Catalyst]=1g/L and power=200W/L) on MB degradation efficiency in sonocatalytic process with 2.5wt% CdSe/GQDs; and (d) Plot of 2.5wt% CdSe/GQDs pHpzc (1g/L).

Impacts of (a) ultrasonic bath power (pH = 9, [MB]0 =20mg/L, and [Catalyst]=1g/L); (b) the presence of radical scavengers (pH = 9, [MB]0 =20mg/L, [Catalyst]=1g/L, power=200W/L, and [scavenger]0 =5mg/L); (c) the presence of various enhancers (pH = 9, [MB]0 =20mg/L, [Catalyst]=1g/L, power=200W/L, and [Enhancer]0 =1mmol/L) on MB degradation efficiency in sonocatalytic process with 2.5wt% CdSe/GQDs; and (d) The activity stability of 2.5wt% CdSe/GQDs nanocatalyst within five repetitive runs.
Suggested sonocatalytic degradation pathway of MB.
Calculated band gap energy of unmodified CdSe and CdSe/GQDs nanostructures.

head.ce:title: Sonocatalytic degradation of methylene blue by a novel graphene quantum dots anchored CdSe nanocatalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Advanced Water and Wastewater Treatment Processes, Department of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, 51666-16471 Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Advanced Water and Wastewater Treatment Processes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanobiotechnology Research Center, Baqiyatallah University of Medical Sciences, 14359-16471 Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Nanotechnology Engineering, Near East University, 99138 Nicosia, North Cyprus, Mersin 10, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Nanotechnology Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Research Laboratory of Advanced Water and Wastewater Treatment Processes, Department of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, 51666-16471 Tabriz, Iran (A. Khataee).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Advanced Water and Wastewater Treatment Processes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cadmium selenide/graphene quantum dots (CdSe/GQDs) nanocatalyst with small band gap energy and a large specific surface area was produced via a facile three-step sonochemical-hydrothermal process. The features of the as-prepared CdSe, GQDs and CdSe/GQDs samples were characterized by photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), energy dispersive X-ray (EDX), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transformed infrared (FT-IR), diffuse-reflectance spectrophotometer (DRS), and Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) analysis. The sonocatalytic activity of the synthesized CdSe/GQDs was effectively accelerated compared with that of pure CdSe nanoparticles in degradation of methylene blue (MB). The influence of the CdSe/GQDs dosage (0.25–1.25g/L), initial MB concentration (20–30mg/L), initial solution pH (3–12), and ultrasonic output power (200–600W/L) were examined on the sonocatalytic treatment of MB aqueous solutions. The degradation efficiency (DE%) of 99% attained at 1g/L of CdSe/GQDs, 20mg/L of MB, pH of 9, and an output power of 200W/L at 90min of ultrasonic irradiation. Furthermore, DE% increased with addition of K2S2O8 and H2O2 as the enhancers via producing more free radicals. However, addition of sulfate, carbonate, and chloride as radical sweeper decreased DE%. Furthermore, well-reusability of the CdSe/GQDs sonocatalyst was demonstrated for 5 successive runs and some of the sonocatalytic generated intermediates were indicated by GC–MS analysis.

ce:section.ce:para:
Methylene blue (MB) can be competently eliminated by advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) without generating any toxic intermediates in its aqueous media [1,2]. Recently, utilization of ultrasonic as an AOP has been attracted a significant consideration. The ultrasonic high-frequency waves are conducted to create and grow micro-sized bubbles which generate very violent pressure and temperature inside the bubbles. This violent situation causes the pyrolysis of H2O and creation of
OH which oxidizes the molecules nearby (Eqs. (1) and (2)). However, the elimination of refractory compounds applying ultrasound consumes vast amounts of energy and its complete degradation is not occurred by using sonolysis alone. This limitation can be dominated by applying an eligible catalyst (sonocatalyst). Pursuant to literature, various kinds of semiconductors (such as TiO2, CdS, and ZnS) are applied as powerful sonocatalyst [3–5].
(1)
H
2
O + Ultrasonic
irradiation →
OH +
H
(2)
OH +
OH →
H
2
O
2

Currently, cadmium selenide (CdSe) has been broadly employed as an efficient semiconductor sonocatalyst for water treatment due to its great efficiency, chemically inertness, and cost-effectivity [6]. CdSe, as a kind of n-type semiconductor, has a band gap energy in the range of 1.65–1.8eV. Moreover, CdSe is an acceptable sonocatalyst owing to the quick creation of electron-hole pairs and high reduction potential electrons [7].

In the recent years, luminescent nanoparticles have engrossed much interest due to their unique sonochemical and sonophysical properties. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are commonly determined as a new group of Zero-dimensional nanoparticles containing mono or few-sheet graphene with dimensions less than 20nm [8]. The great extinction quantities, broad absorption spectrum, nontoxicity, and chemical stability are the most significant specifications [9] which make GQDs as a novel group of potent semiconductor sensitizer that significantly enhances their catalytic activity [10]. As far as we know, there is no study on CdSe sensitizing with GQDs for sonocatalytic applications.

Herein, fine-crystallized CdSe/GQDs were prepared by a three-step hydrothermal method. At the first step, CdSe nanoparticles were prepared then the surface of CdSe nanoparticles was coated with GQDs in the next step that generated from citric acid. Here, GQDs appear as an excellent solid-state electron acceptors and transfer reagent attached on CdSe by in situ sono-assisted tactic under ultrasonic high-frequency irradiation. The resulting CdSe, GQDs and CdSe/GQDs nanocatalyst characterized by PL, SEM, EDX, XRD, FT-IR, DRS, and BET analysis. Moreover, the qualities and enhanced sonocatalytic activity of the CdSe/GQDs (with different loading amounts) were studied on MB elimination. The effects of various parameters such as CdSe/GQDs dosage, initial MB concentration, initial solution pH, ultrasonic output power, enhancers, and radical scavengers on the sonocatalytic treatment of MB aqueous solutions were examined in details. Besides, some of the sonocatalytic generated intermediates were also indicated by GC–MS analysis.

In this study, analytical grade chemicals were used without additional refinement. Cadmium acetate dihydrate (Cd(CH3COO)2·2H2O, 98%), hydrazine monohydrate (N2H4·H2O, 80%), sodium selenite (Na2SeO3, 99%), Ethylene diamine tetra acetic acid (EDTA) (C10H16N2O8Na2·2H2O), NaOH, NaCl, NaHCO3 and K2S2O8 were purchased from Merck. MB and ethanol (90%) was purchased from Shimi Boyakhsaz Co. (Iran) and Iran Daru Co. (Iran), respectively.

For the hydrothermal preparation of CdSe nanoparticles, 1mmol Na2SeO3 and 1mmol NaOH and Cd(CH3COO)2·2H2O were solved in 70mL deionized water. Meanwhile, 1mmol EDTA was solved in 20mL deionized water and added to the above-mentioned solution. The obtained solution stirred at medium speed for 5min then 3mL hydrazine monohydrate was slowly added to the achieved solution. At last, the final solution transferred into a 150mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and located in an oven at 180°C for 24h. The output products were gathered and washed with deionized water and ethanol for three times. Then the obtained black products dried at 50°C for 12h [11].

Here, hydrothermal method was used again for preparation of GQDs. In this method, citric acid (1mmol) and sodium hydroxide (3mmol) were solved in 50mL deionized water, and stirred until solution get clear. Then the obtained solution was transferred into a 100mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and located in an oven at 200°C for 6h. The output product was gathered by adding ethanol and centrifuging at 5000rpm for 20min [12].

The decoration of CdSe nanoparticles with GQDs takes place by a hydrothermal method. 1g powders of formerly prepared CdSe nanoparticles and a defined volume of as-prepared GQDs (0.1mg/mL) colloids (15, 25, 35mL, consistent with to the CdSe/GQDs with GQDs weight content of 1.5, 2.5 and 3.5wt%, respectively) were blended with deionized water to final volume of 50mL. Then, the mixture was sonicated for 1h, transferred into a 100mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and located in an oven at 180°C for 4h. At last, the CdSe/GQDs was gathered and dried in an oven at room temperature for 24h [10].

The photoluminescence spectra (PL) of the as-papered GQDs were recorded by a spectrometer (Perkin Elmer LS45, USA) and the quantum yield (QY) measurement was accomplished by a method reported by Palsson and Monkman [13]. The superficial structure and chemical composition of the CdSe and CdSe/GQDs were defined by SEM and EDX spectroscopy (Mira3 FEG-SEM Tescan, Czech Republic). The crystallinity of the CdSe and CdSe/GQDs were identified by a Siemens X-ray diffractometer (D8 Advance, Bruker, Germany) with Cu-kα radiation (0.15406nm) at an accelerating voltage and current of 40kV and 30mA. IR-spectrometer (Tensor 27, Bruker, Germany) using the KBr pellet method, was used to record the FT-IR spectra. Diffuse reflectance spectrophotometer (S4100, Sinco Co., Korea) was used to obtain diffuse reflectance spectra. The BET analysis took place by a TriStar II Plus instrument using nitrogen adsorption/desorption at 77K (Micromeritics, USA). The zero-charge pH point (pHpzc) of the CdSe/GQDs is the pH which the catalyst does not shift the pH of solution. The pHpzc of the CdSe/GQDs was assigned by the methodology that informed elsewhere [14].

CdSe/GQDs sonocatalytic activity was assessed by MB degradation in aqueous solution under ultrasonic irradiation. For ultrasonic irradiation, the reaction Erlenmeyer flask was placed in an ultrasonic bath (EP S3, 40kHz, 300W, Sonica, Italy). An exact quantity of CdSe/GQDs sonocatalyst was added to MB solution (100mL) with a certain concentration. To obliterate the photocatalytic activity of CdSe/GQDs, the gained solution was sonicated in the dark place. The concentration of the ultrasonically treated MB solution at different process times was calculated by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer at λmax =660nm (Philler Scientific SU 6100, USA).

The as-prepared GQDs colloid exposed a pale-yellow solution (Fig.1 a) and when illuminated with monochromatic 360nm UV beam emitted blue light (460nm, Fig.1b). The UV–Vis spectra of GQDs is represented as Fig.1c, where the absorption bands positioned at 360nm and 460nm. The PL spectra of the GQDs (Fig.1d) demonstrated intense blue emission peak at 440nm under excitation of 340–400nm. The PL spectra showed that no shift occurred in the emission band when excitation wavelength was changed. The blue emission of GQDs was generated due the arranged sp2 clusters (about 1nm) secluded inside the sp3 C–O template [15]. As shown in Scheme 1 , the produced GQDs had plenty of sp2 clusters and oxygen functional groups. Furthermore, the computed QY of the GQDs was ∼9%.

Fig. 2 displays SEM images of the CdSe and CdSe/GQDs nanocatalyst. Fig.2a shows great mass of the CdSe particles which were agglomerated. However, in the CdSe/GQDs nanocatalyst, CdSe particles surface coated by GQDs caused a fine dispersion and avoided the agglomeration of CdSe particles. This fine dispersion could prepare further reactive sites for the reactions (Fig.2b). Furthermore, the electro-conductivity of GQDs could simplify the electron transmission between the CdSe substrate and the adsorbed molecules.

The so-produced CdSe and CdSe/GQDs were analyzed by EDX to investigate the changes in the chemical composition of the nanocatalysts. Fig. 2 (c and d) shows the EDX spectra of CdSe and CdSe/GQDs samples and the corresponding structural element weight percentages of each nanocatalyst. EDX of CdSe/GQDs detected O, C, Cd, and Se as the main elements in the composites which illustrated that GQDs coated on the CdSe particles surfaces.

The XRD patterns of the unmodified CdSe and CdSe/GQDs nanocatalysts are displayed in Fig.3 a. The diffractogram peaks of unmodified CdSe displays peaks about 2θ at 25.54, 42.28, and 50 which are ascribed to (111), (220), and (311) planes of CdSe cubic crystal structure (JCPDS No. 65-2891 for CdSe, cubic) [6]. Obviously, no variant peaks were observed in CdSe/GQDs nanocatalyst compared with unmodified CdSe and the position of the diffractogram peaks did not shift. This clearly demonstrated that the GQDs had no influence on crystal structure of CdSe.

FT-IR spectra were taken to further characterize the as-prepared unmodified CdSe and CdSe/GQDs nanocatalysts (Fig.3b). The FT-IR spectra of CdSe/GQDs show carboxyl group (1386cm−1 and 1600cm−1) and aromatic C–H group (723cm−1) absorption, indicating that the GQDs structure contain carboxyl groups and was successfully formed on the CdSe surface [16].

The BET specific surface area of CdSe and CdSe/GQDs nanocatalysts were measured by the nitrogen gas adsorption–desorption isotherms (Fig.3c). The results showed that the BET specific surface area of CdSe was improved from 10.42 to 22.5 m2/g after modification with GQDs, therefore more active sites were introduced on CdSe/GQDs surface.

MB adsorption on the CdSe/GQDs catalyst (nearly 10% after 150min) can be disregarded for degradation of MB from the solution. The sono-degradation of MB (20mg/L) was studied in the presence and lack of CdSe nanocatalysts under the same operational condition for 150min (Fig. 4 ). The outcomes disclosed that degradation efficiency (DE%) was 13.8%, 33.6%, and 99% for sonolysis without catalyst, sonocatalysis with unmodified CdSe, and CdSe/GQDs nanocatalysts, respectively. Ultrasonic waves can cleave water molecules to produce H
and
OH radicals (Eq. (1)). The
OH radicals are well known to possess oxidizing potential which can attack and destroy almost all organic pollutants to smaller molecules. Nevertheless, the rate of
OH radicals production by sonolysis alone is low. Hence, application of suitable semiconductor catalysts in sonolysis elevated the efficiency of the sonocatalytic process. This enhancement may be ascribed to the growth in number of cavitation bubbles which were formed on the catalyst surface, resulting in more water decomposition and further
OH radical production. Moreover, the solid particles enhance the mass transfer coefficient of contaminants between the liquid and the surface of the sonocatalyst [2,5]. Therefore, the ultrasonic irradiation caused the generation of a wide range of wavelengths, stimulating the sonocatalyst for the formation of numerous oxidizing agents such as
OH,
O2 −, and
OOH on the surface of sonocatalyst (Eqs. (3)–(7)) [6]. Resulted oxidizing agents oxidized organic compounds and enhanced the DE%.
(3)
CdSe
or
CdSe / GQDs + Ultrasonic
irradiation → CdSe
or
CdSe / GQDs (
h
+
+
e
-
)
(4)
h
+
+
H
2
O → 2
OH +
H
+
(5)
h
+
+
HO
-
→
OH
(6)
e
-
+
O
2
→
O
2
-
(7)
H
+
+
O
2
-
→
OOH

Fig. 4 indicates that CdSe/GQDs nanocatalyst had higher sonocatalytic activity than unmodified CdSe nanoparticles. The adhesion of GQDs on the CdSe catalyst resulted in two possible reactions: (i) long-wavelength light absorption and short-wavelength photons emission effect of the GQDs and (ii) the band position of GQDs that enhanced the electron dissociation and amount of available electron transports for reactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of the main operational parameters on the sonocatalysis processes
Impact of the content of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Due to the “sonoluminescence” mechanism, the band gap of sonocatalyst has an important duty in the sonocatalysis processes. Therefore, the band gap energy (Eg) of CdSe and CdSe/GQDs nanocatalysts including various GQDs-content was evaluated via Eq. (8) [17]:
(8)
( Ah
υ
2
) = K ( h υ -
E
g
)
(9)
h υ =
1240
λ
where A (absorption factor) is a constant value, hυ is the photon energy (eV), and K is the autonomous constant. Eg was measured by plotting (Ahυ)2 vs hυ and gained from the linear fitted areas at (Ahυ)2 =0 (Fig.5 a and b). The Eg of CdSe and CdSe/GQDs nanocatalysts including various GQDs-content was reported in Table 1 .

The impact of various amounts of GQDs in sonocatalysis method was investigated by sonocatalytic degradation of MB under the same operational condition (pH=9, [MB]0 =20mg/L, [Cat.]=1g/L and ultrasonic power=200W/L) (Fig.5c). The higher amounts of GQDs than the optimum value resulted in lower activity in sonocatalytic degradation of MB. Furthermore, the depth of light permeation into CdSe significantly exceeded the charge layer space by increment in the amount of GQDs. Moreover, the additional amount of GQDs coating the surface of CdSe nanoparticles caused the electron–hole recombination sites, so the sonocatalytic activity of the catalyst reduced [18].

The impact of CdSe/GQDs dosage on the sonocatalytic degradation of MB was studied by a series of experiments with various CdSe/GQDs dosage from 0.25 to 1.25g/L. As shown in Fig.6 a, the DE% regularly enhanced with increasing the dosage of CdSe/GQDs up to 1g/L, but after that reduced. Increasing the CdSe/GQDs dosage motivated an accountable production of
OH for the degradation of MB. However, at 1.25g/L of CdSe/GQDs the reduction of DE% could be resulted from the aggregation of CdSe/GQDs nanoparticles and thus, reducing the quantity of active sites for the production of
OH. Besides, the collision of activated species with ground state CdSe/GQDs nanoparticles decreased the surficial holes and electrons density. Subsequently, the following experiments were performed with 1g/L of CdSe/GQDs as optimum quantity [19,20].

The impact of initial MB concentration on DE% was investigated in the range of 20 to 30mg/L (Fig.6b). The DE% was reduced from 100% to 64% with increase in the initial MB concentration from 20 to 30mg/L, respectively. This negative effect could be from the followings: (a) absorption of a significant amount of UV light produced from ultrasonic irradiation, by large number of the adsorbed MB molecules on the surface of CdSe/GQDs; (b) reducing the surficial active sites of CdSe/GQDs by MB molecules; (c) increasing the quantity of adsorbed MB molecules. Whereas, for a certain amount of CdSe/GQDs dosage, ultrasonic power, and treatment time, the generated
OH remains constant. The results obviously demonstrated that the DE% was reduced as concurrent with MB concentration [21,22].

The relation between the solution pH and DE% of MB is exposed in Fig.6c. Before beginning irradiation, the pH of the solution was adjusted to 3, 9, and 12. The outcomes confirmed that almost complete degradation of MB was achieved at pH=9, while lower degradation efficiencies of 62%, and 43%, were achieved at pH of 12 and 3, respectively. Owing to the amphoteric feature of the most oxide semiconductors, the surface physics of the semiconductor significantly depend on the pH of the solution and the dimensions of the aggregations. The relation of pH and the sonocatalytic degradation can be described with the point of zero charge (pHPZC) of CdSe/GQDs nanocatalyst (Fig.6d). The pHPZC of as-prepared CdSe/GQDs was about 7.5, this indicates that CdSe/GQDs net charge was zero at pHPZC of 7.5. Similarly, when the pH was less than pHPZC, the surficial charge of CdSe/GQDs is positive and vice versa.

The surface of the catalyst adsorbed MB as a cathodic dye at higher pH than 7.5. On the other hand, the decrease in DE% at pH=12 could be attributed to the electrostatic interdiction among surficial negative charge of CdSe/GQDs and OH−, because of this reason the creation of
OH was reduced. At pH=3 the electrostatic interdiction among surficial positive charge of CdSe/GQDs and MB molecules reduced the adsorption of MB on the surface of CdSe/GQDs nanocatalyst; Moreover, according to Eq. (4), the high quantity of H+ reduced the generation of
OH [17,23].

Fig.7 a displays the impact of ultrasonic bath power on the degradation of MB by CdSe/GQDs ([MB]0 =20mg/L and [CdSe/GQDs]=1g/L). Elevating the ultrasonic bath power from 200 to 600W/L improved the DE% of MB from 55% to 99% in 30min of reaction time, respectively. The quantity of crumpling bubbles increased by elevating the ultrasonic bath power, so the generation of
OH and subsequently DE% of MB was improved [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of the presence of radical scavengers
ce:section.ce:para:
Generally, aqueous solutions contain ionic species such as chloride (Cl−), sulfate (SO4 2−), and carbonate (CO3 2−) which affect the activity of sonocatalytic degradation due to their radical scavenging effect. The radical scavenging impact were examined in the presence of sodium chloride, sodium sulfate, and sodium carbonate with sonocatalytic degradation of MB (pH=9, [MB]0 =20mg/L, [cat.]=1g/L, power=200W/L, and [scavenger]0 =5mg/L) (Fig.7b). The results demonstrated that the DE% of MB reduced in the attendance of all examined ionic scavengers, approving that the attack of the generated radical species was the prevalent mechanism of the sonocatalytic degradation of organic compound. As shown in Fig7b, chloride ions had the most scavenging impact on DE% among the examined radical scavengers. The possible mechanism of the various radical scavengers can be described as Eqs. (10)–(12) [24–26]:
(10)
Cl
-
+
OH → Cl
+
OH
-
(11)
SO
4
2 -
+
OH →
SO
4
-
+
OH
-
(12)
CO
3
2 -
+
OH →
CO
3
-
+
OH
-

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of the presence of various enhancers
ce:section.ce:para:
The impact of different oxidant such as hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and Peroxydisulfate anion (S2O8 2−) on the sonocatalytic degradation of MB (pH=9, [MB]0 =20mg/L, [Catalyst]=1g/L, power=200W/L, and [Enhancer]0 =1mmol/L) was investigated and the result is demonstrated in Fig.7c. Results displayed that the addition of H2O2 and S2O8 2− improved the DE% of MB from 55% to 66% and 83% in 30min of reaction time, respectively. In the case of the addition of H2O2, the ultrasonic irradiation decomposes H2O2 to
OH as explained in Eqs. (13) and (14), so in the presence of H2O2 the DE% of MB was enhanced [24,25].
(13)
H
2
O
2
+ Ultrasonic
irradiation → 2
OH
(14)
H
2
O
2
+
H →
H
2
O +
OH

On the other hand, the addition of S2O8 2− enhanced the sonocatalytic DE%. S2O8 2− is a strong oxidant (E°=2.01V) that can be converted to sulfate radicals (SO4
−) as a stronger oxidant (E°=2.6V), by transition metal ions, UV irradiation, thermal energy or ultrasound irradiation (Eqs. (15) and (16)). Moreover, the formed SO4
− can take part in the degradation of organic compounds as explained in Eqs. (17) and (18) [27].
(15)
S
2
O
8
2 -
+ Ultrasonic
irradiation → 2
SO
4
-
(16)
SO
4
-
+
H
2
O →
H
+
+
SO
4
2 -
+
OH
(17)
SO
4
-
+ RH →
SO
4
2 -
+ R
( intermediates ) +
H
+
(18)
SO
4
-
+
R
( intermediates ) →
SO
4
2 -
+
CO
2
+
NO
2
+ other
inorganics

The activity stability of 5% CdSe/GQDs nanocatalyst was tried for five repetitive runs ([MB]0 =20mg/L, [Catalyst]=1g/L, pH=9, power=200W/L). The small reduction in the DE% (Fig.7d) illustrated that CdSe/GQDs nanocatalyst had high activity stability and can be recycled more than five times without considerable reduction in its sonocatalytic activity.
The by-products produced through the sonocatalytic degradation of MB were investigated by GC–MS and the suggested degradation pathway presented in Scheme 2 .

The by-products identified by matching with standard spectrums. Creation of these by-products during the sonocatalytic degradation of MB approved efficient oxidation of MB by the studied sonocatalytic degradation procedure [28].

In summary, GQDs colloids were prepared as efficient sensitizers of CdSe nanoparticles to obtain a sonosensitive nanocatalyst. The prepared CdSe/GQDs nanocatalysts were characterized by SEM, EDX, XRD, FT-IR, DRS, PL and BET analysis. The CdSe/GQDs nanocatalysts used in sonocatalytic degradation of MB in aqueous solution. The outcomes demonstrated that the sonocatalysis had higher DE% compared with sonolysis alone, and DE% was obviously affected by the content of GQDs in CdSe/GQD. Furthermore, H2O2 and S2O8 2− enhanced the DE%. The different ionic radical scavengers decreased the DE%, indicating that the main agent of sonocatalytic degradation of MB was the free radicals. The highest sonocatalytic DE% was detected at pH=9. The increasing in ultrasonic power caused an enhancement in the DE% of MB. Besides, CdSe/GQDs nanocatalyst displayed great stability and reusability, which is an important feature for catalysts.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the University of Tabriz/Iran and Iran Science Elites Federation for their support. We also thank Dr. Shima Rahim Pouran for her helpful comments.
